content.method: Longitudinal tracking of enrollment shifts in districts receiving significant comprehensive-school investment versus matched comparison districts; survey-based decomposition of parental choice motivations (quality perception versus prestige/status signaling) using conjoint analysis.
content.question: Can compensatory investment in comprehensive-school quality, absent any admissions or structural change, meaningfully shift enrollment demand away from selective tracks within a politically and demographically constrained timeframe?
content.why_it_matters: T4 is the least politically contentious and legally simplest proposal, but its core causal premise (quality investment reduces selective-track demand) is unverified and contested by evidence suggesting demand is partly status-driven rather than quality-driven. This determines whether T4 can function as a standalone strategy or only as a necessary complement to structural reform.
